summary: Gruber dismantles the conventional wisdom that Apple's failure to license the Macintosh in the 1980s was a catastrophic strategic error. He argues that IBM-compatible PCs were technically incapable of running a Mac-like OS in 1984, making software-only licensing impossible. He reframes the key difference between Apple and Microsoft not as open-vs-closed licensing, but as pragmatism-vs-idealism: Microsoft parlayed each success into the next (DOS to Windows to Office), while Apple repeatedly built revolutionary but incompatible new platforms (Apple II to Mac to Newton). The Mac's low market share likely stems not from licensing decisions but from Apple's failure to parlay the Apple II's success, though doing so would have produced something far less revolutionary.
key_points: IBM-compatible PCs in 1984 were technically incapable of providing a Mac-like experience — much of the Mac OS was in ROM and depended on proprietary hardware, making software-only licensing impossible.

The real strategic difference between Apple and Microsoft was not open-vs-closed but pragmatism-vs-idealism: Microsoft relentlessly parlayed each success into the next, while Apple built revolutionary but incompatible new platforms.

Microsoft's Windows monopoly is an anomaly born from IBM's decision to license DOS rather than proof that markets inherently favor open platforms — as evidenced by closed console gaming platforms like Nintendo, Sony, and Xbox.
Apple repeated the pattern of non-parlaying with the Newton, which had only tenuous ties to the Mac instead of being designed as a must-have Mac peripheral.
A hypothetical Mac built on the Apple II would have been evolutionary rather than revolutionary — pragmatic but fundamentally un-Apple.
money_quotes: Toss it back and forth long enough and a nugget of conventional wisdom eventually comes to be treated as fact.
Companies which compete directly against Microsoft have a tendency to go out of business quickly and gruesomely.
Microsoft parlayed their DOS command-line-era monopoly into the Windows GUI-era monopoly, then parlayed their Windows monopoly into the Office monopoly.
That they ended up on different sides of the open/proprietary divide is an effect, not the cause.
The difference between Microsoft and Apple wasn't about open-vs.-closed; it was pragmatism-vs.-idealism.
The real Mac was revolutionary; a hypothetical Mac based on the Apple II would have been evolutionary.
We might have ended up with something merely 'new and improved', which isn't nearly as exciting as 'insanely great'.
I posit that the Windows monopoly is an anomaly, and exists only because of IBM's decision to license the DOS operating system from Microsoft, rather than buying it or writing their own from scratch.
key_insight: The Mac's low market share resulted not from Apple's refusal to license but from their idealistic refusal to parlay existing successes into new platforms — a pattern that produced revolutionary products at the cost of incremental market dominance.
